frameworks.name: resting-state functional connectivity
frameworks.one_line: whole-brain rs-fMRI correlations used to map hemispheric lateralization at the connection level

frameworks.name: task-positive vs task-negative network dichotomy
frameworks.one_line: separates anticorrelated networks to avoid spuriously inflated lateralization indices

frameworks.name: k-means clustering
frameworks.one_line: data-driven grouping of connections to identify left- and right-lateralized sets

core_question: How is functional connectivity lateralized across the whole brain when analyzed within task-positive and task-negative networks?
key_assumptions: negative correlations between task-positive and task-negative regions would inflate lateralization if mixed
402 equally spaced ROIs adequately cover the gray matter
lateralization is meaningfully defined at the level of individual functional connections
manipulations: partitioning ROIs into 358 task-positive and 44 task-negative regions
computing lateralization separately within each network
clustering connections into left- vs right-lateralized sets
key_findings: right-lateralized task-positive connections link occipital with inferior/middle frontal regions
left-lateralized task-positive connections involve fusiform with inferior frontal and inferior/superior parietal regions
task-negative left-lateralized connections primarily link precuneus with medial prefrontal regions
mean lateralization indices of left- and right-lateralized connections are correlated
interpretations: lateralization of functional connectivity may arise from complementary processes between the two lateralized networks
